summary:
Sher Shah Suri (r. 1540–1545 AD) was an Afghan chieftain who briefly overthrew the [[event:103|Mughal Empire]] and established the Sur Empire, ruling for only five years before his death in an accidental gunpowder explosion — yet accomplishing more administrative innovation in that brief period than many rulers achieve in decades. Sher Shah rose from a minor official in the Bihar region through military service and strategic marriages, built a power base in Bengal and Bihar, and twice defeated [[event:3233|Humayun]] (at Chausa in 1539 and Kannauj in 1540), forcing the Mughal emperor into exile and seizing Delhi. His administrative reforms during his short reign created structures that the restored Mughals would inherit and build upon — making him, paradoxically, one of the greatest contributors to the Mughal administrative tradition despite being their enemy.

Sher Shah's most lasting achievement was the Grand Trunk Road — the great highway stretching from Bengal to Kabul that he rebuilt and extended, lined with shade trees and provided with caravanserais (rest houses) every two kos (approximately 4 km). This road, which Rudyard Kipling would later call 'the backbone of all Hind,' created the communications and commercial infrastructure that unified northern India and remained in use for centuries. His administrative innovations included a standardized currency (the silver rupee became the standard monetary unit), a comprehensive land survey and revenue assessment system, a postal system (dak chowki), and a standardized system of weights and measures — all inherited and elaborated by Akbar. Sher Shah died in 1545 when a gunpowder magazine exploded during the siege of Kalinjar, a fitting end for a man who had risen through gunpowder warfare.
